headline: The Trout-Bryant Cold War, Gary Sánchez in Round 7, and the Most Chaotic Draft in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball History
subheadline: Fourteen teams, 210 picks, one future MVP sliding to pick 62, and at least three managers who should probably have their draft privileges revoked.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

Every fantasy baseball draft has a moment—usually somewhere between rounds 4 and 7—when the room (or in 2017's case, the group chat) goes completely sideways. Someone reaches for a closer. Someone autodrafts because their kid needed a diaper change. Someone takes Carlos González when J.D. Martinez is sitting right there like a $100 bill on the sidewalk. That's when you know: this is real. This is MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball, where the stakes are a case of beer and eternal bragging rights, but somehow it feels like we're all auditioning for a job at the Dodgers' analytics department.

This year's draft felt like watching Season 1 of *The Wire*—methodical, occasionally brilliant, frequently frustrating, and featuring at least three characters you wanted to yell at through the screen. We had **Mike Trout** going first overall (obviously), **Gary Sánchez** falling to Round 7 like he was invisible (WHAT?!), and someone drafting **Hunter Pence** in Round 15 like it was 2012 and he hadn't just posted a .671 OPS. The steals were real. The reaches were painful. And somewhere in the middle rounds, championships were won and lost before a single pitch was thrown.

Let me walk you through all of it—the good, the bad, and the inexplicable decisions that made me question whether some of these managers actually watch baseball or just refresh ESPN's homepage once a week.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The first round played out almost exactly how you'd expect if you put 14 reasonably intelligent fantasy managers in a room with access to the same rankings. **Mike Trout** went 1.1 (duh), **Kris Bryant** went 1.2 (solid), **Manny Machado** at 1.3 (fine), **Mookie Betts** at 1.4 (love it). The first real decision point came at 1.5, where **Team t.6** went with **Clayton Kershaw**—the first pitcher off the board. In a 12-category league with IP, K, ERA, WHIP, QS, and NSVH, you can make a case for anchoring your staff with a certified ace. Kershaw's 2016 was absurd: 1.69 ERA, 0.73 WHIP, 11.6 K/9. That's not a pitcher, that's a cheat code. Still, taking a pitcher over **Nolan Arenado** (who went 1.6) or **Paul Goldschmidt** (1.7) is a choice you have to live with.

The late first round got interesting. **Jose Altuve** at 1.9 felt right—dude hit .338 with 30 steals and won the batting title. But **Carlos Correa** at 1.10 over **Josh Donaldson**? Donaldson fell all the way to 2.11, which is either a steal or an indictment of everyone who passed on the reigning MVP runner-up. (Spoiler: it's a steal.)

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**Team t.9 (Draft Pick #1)**

You have the first overall pick, you take **Mike Trout**, you feel good about yourself. That's the easy part. But this roster goes deeper than expected. **Corey Seager** at 2.20 is solid value for a shortstop who hit .308 with 26 homers and a 138 wRC+ as a 22-year-old rookie (finished second in MVP voting, by the way). **Jon Lester** at 3.21 gives them a workhorse who logged 202.2 innings with a 2.44 ERA. The middle rounds got weird, though—**Jacob deGrom** at 7.61 is either a massive steal or an injury concern (his 2016 was limited but he posted a 3.04 ERA when healthy). **Andrew Miller** at 9.81 is perfect RP value in a league that counts NSVH. The late-round picks (**Steven Matz**, **Adam Eaton**, **Hunter Pence**) feel like they're hoping for 2015 versions of these players, which... good luck with that.

**Grade: B+** — Trout carries any roster, but the depth here is quietly excellent if deGrom's elbow cooperates.

**Team t.2 (Draft Pick #2)**

This might be my favorite draft in the league. **Kris Bryant** at 1.2 is a no-brainer—dude put up 39 homers and 7.7 WAR while playing Gold Glove-caliber defense at third. But then they grabbed **Noah Syndergaard** at 2.19 (2.60 ERA, 218 strikeouts in 183.2 IP) and **Corey Kluber** at 3.22 (3.14 ERA, 227 Ks). That's a 1-2 rotation punch that could dominate any pitching category week. **Gary Sánchez** at 7.62 is borderline criminal—the kid hit 20 homers in 53 games with a .657 slugging percentage in his rookie season. FIFTY-THREE GAMES! He should've been a Round 4 pick minimum. **DJ LeMahieu** at 9.82 gives them the 2016 batting champ (.348 average) as a backup middle infielder. **Edwin Díaz** at 10.99 is a speculative closer play that could pay off huge.

**Grade: A** — The Bryant-Syndergaard-Kluber-Sánchez spine is championship-caliber. This team drafted like they actually did research.

**Team t.10 (Draft Pick #3)**

**Manny Machado** at 1.3 anchors things nicely—37 homers, 96 RBI, plays a premium position. But then they went full ace-collector: **Madison Bumgarner** at 2.18 and **Chris Sale** at 3.23. Sale had a 3.34 ERA with 233 strikeouts for the White Sox in 2016; Bumgarner was similarly dominant (2.74 ERA). That's an elite rotation foundation, but did they sacrifice hitting depth? **Jean Segura** at 4.38 is interesting—he hit .319 with 33 steals for Arizona—but **Jonathan Villar** at 5.43 feels like a reach for a guy whose .285/.369/.457 line might've been a career year. **Mark Trumbo** at 8.78 is a pure power play (47 homers in 2016, leading the league). The bullpen depth with **Mark Melancon** and **Jeurys Familia** is smart. This team is built to win pitching categories and pray for Trumbo dongs.

**Grade: B** — The top-heavy pitching is beautiful, but the hitting after Machado gets thin fast.

**Team t.8 (Draft Pick #4)**

Opening with **Mookie Betts** at 1.4 is *chef's kiss*—the man hit .318 with 31 homers, 26 steals, and played elite defense in 2016. He's a five-category monster. Following that with **Miguel Cabrera** (2.17) and **Joey Votto** (3.24) gives them a murderer's row of first basemen... except now they have a roster construction problem. Miggy's .316/38/108 line is still elite, and Votto's .326/29/97 with a .434 OBP is *preposterous*, but how do you play both plus Betts? Then they took **Nelson Cruz** at 4.37 (43 homers, 119 wRC+) and **AJ Pollock** at 5.44, who missed most of 2016 with a fractured elbow. This feels like they're collecting cards instead of building a team. **Stephen Strasburg** at 6.57 (15-4, 3.60 ERA) gives them an ace, and the pitching depth (**Kyle Hendricks**, **Carlos Martínez**, **Jose Quintana**) is sneaky good. But the positional flexibility concerns me.

**Grade: B-** — Elite talent, questionable fit. It's like they drafted with a spreadsheet that only had wRC+ and ignored roster construction.

**Team t.6 (Draft Pick #5)**

The **Clayton Kershaw** pick at 1.5 was bold, and I respect it. When your ace has a 1.69 ERA and 0.73 WHIP, you're basically getting a category win every week. **Trea Turner** at 2.16 is fascinating—he only played 73 games in 2016 but hit .342 with 33 steals in that limited time. High-risk, high-reward stuff. **Jake Arrieta** at 3.25 pairs with Kershaw beautifully (3.10 ERA, 190 Ks). But here's where it gets spicy: **George Springer** at 4.36, **Giancarlo Stanton** at 5.45, and **Kyle Schwarber** at 6.56. Stanton played only 119 games due to injury, but his power (27 HR, .489 SLG in limited action) is still top-tier. Schwarber missed almost all of 2016 with a torn ACL—this is a pure speculation pick. **Alex Bregman** at 10.96 is the sneaky genius move; the kid hit .264 with 8 homers in 49 big league games and has star upside.

**Grade: B+** — Boom-or-bust roster built on aces and injured upside. If Stanton and Schwarber are healthy, watch out.

**Team t.3 (Draft Pick #6)**

**Nolan Arenado** at 1.6 is just steady excellence—.294/41/133 in 2016, playing half his games at Coors. **Max Scherzer** at 2.15 (20-7, 2.96 ERA, 284 Ks) gives them the most dominant strikeout pitcher in baseball. **Freddie Freeman** at 3.26 is underrated value—the guy hit .302 with 34 homers and should've been a Round 2 consideration. The middle picks are where this draft shines: **Brian Dozier** at 4.35 (42 homers from the two-hole, absolutely insane power for a second baseman), **Christian Yelich** at 7.66 (.298 average, excellent OBP, 21 steals), and **J.T. Realmuto** at 15.146 (criminally undervalued catcher who hit .303). The **José Bautista** pick at 9.86 is aging-star risk, but he still hit 22 homers despite declining metrics. **Dansby Swanson** and **Addison Russell** in the late rounds give them cheap MI depth.

**Grade: A-** — Arenado-Scherzer-Freeman-Dozier is a murderer's row core. If Yelich takes another step forward, this team could win it all.

**Team t.5 (Draft Pick #7)**

**Paul Goldschmidt** at 1.7 is rock-solid—.297/24/95 with 32 steals from a first baseman. **Xander Bogaerts** at 2.14 gives them a shortstop who hit .294 and was one of the best pure hitters at his position. But the pitching anchor is concerning: **Johnny Cueto** at 3.27 had a great 2016 (2.79 ERA), but the injury history is real. **Edwin Encarnación** at 4.34 provides monster power (42 HR, 127 RBI), but now they have two first basemen again. **Rougned Odor** at 5.47 is a polarizing pick—33 homers but also a .296 OBP that kills you in that category. **Rick Porcello** at 8.74 is the reigning Cy Young winner (22-4, 3.15 ERA), but we all know that award was partly a team-record mirage. **Evan Longoria**, **Adam Jones**, **Justin Upton**—this is a roster of solid veterans who might all decline simultaneously.

**Grade: B-** — Goldschmidt and Encarnación provide a solid floor, but I see too many aging bats and shaky pitching depth.

**Team t.1 (Draft Pick #8)**

**Bryce Harper** at 1.8 is interesting because his 2016 was *weird*—.243/24/86 with a .373 OBP. That's a far cry from his 2015 MVP season (.330/42/99). But we're betting on the talent, right? **Charlie Blackmon** at 2.13 is a borderline steal; the guy hit .324 with 29 homers and 17 steals at Coors. **Ryan Braun** at 3.28 (30 HR, .538 SLG) is steady veteran production. **Yu Darvish** at 4.33 returns from Tommy John surgery—high risk, elite upside if healthy. **Buster Posey** at 5.48 gives them the best catcher in baseball (.288/14/80, .434 OBP). The bullpen is stacked: **Aroldis Chapman** (6.53), **Wade Davis** (9.88), **Craig Kimbrel** (10.93). That's three elite closers in a league that counts saves and holds. **Justin Turner** at 11.108 is the sneakiest pick—dude hit .275 with a .339 OBP and could be due for a breakout.

**Grade: B+** — If Harper returns to MVP form and Darvish is healthy, this roster has the ceiling of a champion. Those are two massive ifs.

**Team t.4 (Draft Pick #9)**

**Jose Altuve** at 1.9 is the safest pick in fantasy—.338/24/96 with 30 steals, impossible to whiff on. **Anthony Rizzo** at 2.12 (.292/32/109) gives them another elite bat. **Robinson Canó** at 3.29 (.298/39/103) had a monster 2016 for Seattle. That's a top-three core that should compete in every hitting category. **Justin Verlander** at 4.32 (16-9, 3.04 ERA) anchors the staff nicely. **Yoenis Céspedes** at 5.49 (31 HR) is power with injury risk. **Billy Hamilton** at 9.89 is a one-category specialist—59 steals, but a .260/.321/.343 line that murders your batting average weeks. The **Roberto Osuna** pick at 11.109 (36 saves) is smart RP value. **Dallas Keuchel** at 14.132 is a rebound candidate after his 4.55 ERA; we know he can pitch (he won the Cy Young in 2015).

**Grade: B+** — Altuve-Rizzo-Canó is lethal. If Verlander has one more great year left, this team is dangerous.

**Team t.7 (Draft Pick #10)**

Having pick 10 in a snake draft means you also get pick 11, which is a cheat code. **Carlos Correa** (1.10) and **Josh Donaldson** (2.11) in back-to-back picks? Yes please. Correa hit .274/20/96 with 13 steals as a 21-year-old; Donaldson put up .284/37/99 with a 5.7 WAR despite a down (by his standards) season. Then they grabbed **Francisco Lindor** at 3.30 (.301/15/78, 19 steals)—another elite shortstop. Three elite infielders to start is a strategy I respect. **Starling Marte** at 4.31 (.311/9/46, 47 steals) is a stolen base machine. **J.D. Martinez** at 5.50 (.307/22/68 in 120 games) is a sneaky power bat who was undervalued because of injury history. The pitching is prospect-heavy: **Gerrit Cole** (10.91), **Aaron Sanchez** (11.110), **Jameson Taillon** (13.130), **Aaron Nola** (15.150). That's a lot of upside, but also a lot of risk.

**Grade: A-** — The Correa-Donaldson-Lindor trifecta is the best infield core in the league. If even two of those young arms hit, look out.

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE THREE BEST VALUE PICKS:**

1. **Gary Sánchez, Round 7, Pick 62 (Team t.2)** — This is the kind of pick that wins leagues. Sánchez hit 20 homers with a .657 slugging percentage in 53 games. FIFTY-THREE GAMES! He should've been gone by Round 4 at the latest. The catcher position is a wasteland, and Team t.2 just grabbed a potential top-5 player at the position for free. This is like finding a $20 bill in your jacket pocket, except the jacket is made of baseballs and the $20 bill is actually $200.

2. **Josh Donaldson, Round 2, Pick 11 (Team t.7)** — The 2015 AL MVP put up 37 homers and 5.7 WAR in a "down year" and somehow fell to the second round. Did everyone just forget he exists? He was the 2.11 pick, meaning Team t.7 got Correa AND Donaldson in consecutive selections. That's criminal.

3. **Alex Bregman, Round 10, Pick 96 (Team t.6)** — A top prospect who already showed he could hit at the big league level (.264/8 HR in 49 games) sliding to the 10th round is absurd. This is the kind of pick that looks genius in hindsight when he's putting up 30-homer seasons.

**THE THREE WORST REACHES:**

1. **Jonathan Villar, Round 5, Pick 43 (Team t.10)** — Look, I get it. Villar had 62 steals in 2016. But his .369 OBP was 60 points higher than his career mark, and his .285 average felt fluky. Taking him over safer options in Round 5 is betting on the outlier instead of the player. (Also, his 174 strikeouts should've been a red flag.)

2. **Hunter Pence, Round 15, Pick 141 (Team t.9)** — Pence missed most of 2016 with hamstring issues and hit .289/.357/.451 in limited action. But he's 34, injury-prone, and his best days are clearly behind him. Even in Round 15, there were better upside plays.

3. **Billy Hamilton, Round 9, Pick 89 (Team t.4)** — Hamilton's 59 steals are nice, but his .260/.321/.343 slash line actively hurts you in three categories (H/AB, RBI, and probably OBP depending on your other hitters). He's a one-tool player you're spending a Round 9 pick on. That's too rich for a category specialist.

**V. Five Bold Predictions for 2017**

1. **Team t.2 wins the championship**, and it won't be particularly close. The Bryant-Syndergaard-Kluber-Sánchez core is the best in the league, and they have no obvious weaknesses. This is the Patriots of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball.

2. **Gary Sánchez finishes as a top-3 catcher** and everyone who passed on him in Rounds 2-6 has to explain themselves at next year's draft. The man is a monster, and Round 7 was a gift.

3. **Team t.1's bullpen strategy pays off big time.** Chapman, Wade Davis, and Kimbrel give them a stranglehold on saves and holds. In a league that counts NSVH, that's three categories they're winning most weeks.

4. **At least two of Team t.7's young pitchers (Cole, Sanchez, Taillon, Nola) have breakout seasons**, making their prospect-heavy strategy look genius. The downside is real, but so is the upside.

5. **Kyle Schwarber's ADP gamble fails for Team t.6.** Coming back from a torn ACL, his rusty timing means a slow start, and he never regains his 2015 form. The pick made sense on paper, but sometimes paper lies.

**VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

I've gone back and forth on this more than I should admit, but I keep coming back to **Team t.2**. The math is simple: **Kris Bryant** is a top-5 overall player. **Noah Syndergaard** and **Corey Kluber** give them two aces in a league where pitching categories are half the battle. **Gary Sánchez** in Round 7 is the kind of value that tilts entire seasons. And they've got **Carlos González** and **Trevor Story** providing upside in the middle rounds, plus **DJ LeMahieu** as the batting champ insurance policy.

This is a team built for a 12-category league. They can win batting average, runs, homers, and RBI with their hitting core. They can dominate ERA, WHIP, Ks, and QS with Syndergaard and Kluber anchoring the rotation. They've got **Edwin Díaz** for future saves upside. The construction is just... smart. It's the fantasy equivalent of the 2004 Patriots—not the flashiest roster, but every piece fits.

Team t.2 drafts like they actually understand how snake drafts work: take the best player available, stack your strengths, and don't reach for closers in Round 4. They'll be holding the trophy in September, and the rest of us will be wondering why we let Gary Sánchez fall to pick 62.

See you at the waiver wire.

— Bill

*(This column brought to you by MillerLite®. It's only beer league if you're drinking.)*
